questions.question_html: Do magnets stick to objects made of certain materials only?
questions.solution_html:
Yes, magnets only stick to certain materials.
These materials are primarily those that contain iron, nickel, or cobalt, or a combination of these elements. These materials are called ferromagnetic materials.
Other materials, like wood, plastic, and glass, are not attracted to magnets.

questions.question_html: Do all parts of a magnet attract magnetic materials equally?
questions.solution_html:
No, all parts of a magnet do not attract magnetic materials equally.   
The magnetic force is strongest at the poles (north and south) of a magnet. As you move towards the center of the magnet, the magnetic force weakens. This is why if you cut a magnet in half, you'll end up with two new magnets, each with its own north and south pole.

questions.question_html: Can we find a magnet with a single pole?
questions.solution_html:
No, we cannot find a magnet with a single pole.
This is a fundamental law of magnetism. Magnets always have both a north and a south pole, no matter how small you cut them.
While there are theories about the existence of magnetic monopoles (particles with only one magnetic pole), none have been experimentally confirmed yet.

questions.question_html: What direction does this line indicate along which the magnet rests? How can we find it out?
questions.solution_html:
A Freely Suspended Magnet Points North-South
A freely suspended magnet, like a compass needle, will always align itself in a north-south direction.
How to Determine the Direction

  1. Suspend the magnet: Tie a thin, non-magnetic thread to the center of the magnet and hang it from a fixed point, allowing it to swing freely without any disturbances.
  2. Observe the alignment: Once the magnet stops swinging, the end pointing towards the geographic north is considered the north pole of the magnet.

Why does this happen?
The Earth itself behaves like a giant magnet with its south magnetic pole near the geographic north and vice versa. The opposite poles of magnets attract each other, so the north pole of the magnet is drawn towards the Earth's geographic north.

questions.question_html: Unlike poles of two magnets ________ each other, whereas like poles ________ each other.
The needle of a magnetic compass rests along the ________ direction.
A magnet always has ________ poles.
The materials that are attracted towards a magnet are called ________.
A magnet can be broken into pieces to obtain a single pole.
Iron filings mostly stick in the middle of a bar magnet when it is brought near them.
A freely suspended bar magnet always aligns with the north-south direction.
Similar poles of a magnet repel each other.

Column I shows different positions in which one pole of a magnet is placed near that of the other. Column II indicates the resulting interaction between them for different situations. Fill in the blanks.

Column I Column II
N - N ________
N - ________ Attraction
S - N ________
________ - S Repulsion
questions.solution_html:
Column I Column II
N - N Repulsion
N - S Attraction
S - N Attraction
S - S Repulsion

questions.question_html:
Atharv performed an experiment in which he took a bar magnet and rolled it over a heap of steel U-clips

According to you, which of the options given in Table is likely to be his observation?

Number of pins attracted by the magnet at its various positions

  Position A Position B Position C
(i) 10 2 10
(ii) 10 10 2
(iii) 2 10 10
(iv) 10 10 10
questions.solution_html:
In Atharv's experiment, the bar magnet will attract the most U-clips at the poles (positions A and C) and the least at the middle (position B). This is because the magnetic force is strongest at the poles and weakest at the middle.

  1. Position A (North Pole): High attraction
  2. Position B (Middle): Low attraction
  3. Position C (South Pole): High attraction

So, the observation (i) 10, 2, 10 will be his observation.

questions.question_html: Reshma bought three identical metal bars from the market. Out of these bars, two were magnets and one was just a piece of iron. How will she identify which two amongst the three could be magnets (without using any other material)?
questions.solution_html: Reshma can identify the magnets by bringing each metal bar close to the other two bars one by one. The two magnets will attract the iron bar but will repel or attract each other depending on their poles. If a bar repels one bar and attracts another, it indicates that the repelling bar is also a magnet. The one that only attracts and does not repel is the piece of iron.

questions.question_html: You are given a magnet which does not have the poles marked. How can you find its poles with the help of another magnet which has its poles marked?
questions.solution_html: To find the poles of an unmarked magnet, bring one end of the marked magnet near one end of the unmarked magnet. If they attract, the unmarked magnet's end is the opposite pole (i.e., if the marked North pole attracts, then the unmarked end is South pole). If they repel, then the poles are similar.

questions.question_html: A bar magnet has no markings to indicate its poles. How would you find out near which end its North pole is located without using another magnet?
questions.solution_html: Suspend the magnet freely using a string. The end that points towards the north direction is the North pole of the magnet.

questions.question_html: If the earth is itself a magnet, can you guess the poles of earth’s magnet by looking at the direction of the magnetic compass?
questions.solution_html: Yes, we can guess the poles of Earth's magnet by looking at the direction of the magnetic compass. The north-seeking end of the compass needle points towards the geographic North Pole of the Earth, which is actually the magnetic South pole of the Earth's magnet.

questions.question_html: While a mechanic was repairing a gadget using a screw driver, the steel screws kept falling down. Suggest a way to solve the problem of the mechanic on the basis of what you have learnt in this chapter.
questions.solution_html: The mechanic can magnetize the screwdriver by repeatedly stroking it with one pole of a magnet from one end to the other in a single direction. This will make the screwdriver magnetic and help attract and hold the steel screws.

questions.question_html: Two ring magnets X and Y are arranged as shown in Fig. It is observed that the magnet X does not move down further. What could be the possible reason? Suggest a way to bring the magnet X in contact with magnet Y, without pushing either of the magnets.
questions.solution_html:
The reason magnet X does not move down further is due to magnetic repulsion. The like poles (either both North or both South) of the ring magnets X and Y are facing each other, causing a repulsive force that prevents X from moving closer to Y.
To bring magnet X in contact with magnet Y without pushing either of the magnets, remove one magnet (Lift magnet X off the rod). Now flip magnet X (Turn magnet X upside down, reversing its poles) and replace magnet X (Slide magnet X back onto the rod).
By flipping magnet X, the opposite poles of the two magnets will face each other (one North and one South), which will cause an attractive force, allowing magnet X to move down and contact magnet Y.
This method utilises the principle of magnetic attraction and repulsion to achieve the desired outcome without any external force.

questions.question_html: Three magnets are arranged on a table in the form of the shape shown in Fig. What is the polarity, N or S, at the ends 1, 2, 3, 4 and 6 of the magnets? Polarity of one end (5) is given for you.
questions.solution_html:
Based on the figure given here and knowing that magnetic poles always come in pairs (North and South), we can deduce the polarities at the ends:
Given: End 5 is North (N).
Knowing that the opposite end of a magnet is the opposite pole, the polarity at end 6, being the opposite end of 5, will be South (S).
Now, considering how the magnets are arranged:
The end 5 is North, and magnetically, the polarity flow is from North to South inside the magnet. So, the end connected to 5 (which is end 4) must be South.
Since magnet 3 is connected directly to end 4:
End 3 (opposite to end 4) will be North (N).
The end connected to 3 (which is end 2) will be South (S).
Finally, the polarities can be stated as follows:

  • End 1: North (N)
  • End 2: South (S)
  • End 3: North (N)
  • End 4: South (S)
  • End 6: South (S)
